elements.sent-2411-001: With the increasing sophistication of Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs), the demand for effective detection and mitigation strategies and methods has escalated.
elements.sent-2411-002: Program execution leaves traces in the system audit log, which can be analyzed to detect malicious activities.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, collecting and analyzing large volumes of audit logs over extended periods is challenging, further compounded by insufficient labeling that hinders their usability.
elements.sent-2411-004: Addressing these challenges, this paper introduces SAGA (Synthetic Audit log Generation for APT campaigns), a novel approach for generating find-grained labeled synthetic audit logs that mimic real-world system logs while embedding stealthy APT attacks.
elements.sent-2411-005: SAGA generates configurable audit logs for arbitrary duration, blending benign logs from normal operations with malicious logs based on the definitions the MITRE ATT\&CK framework.
elements.sent-2411-006: Malicious audit logs follow an APT lifecycle, incorporating various attack techniques at each stage.
elements.sent-2411-007: These synthetic logs can serve as benchmark datasets for training machine learning models and assessing diverse APT detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-008: To demonstrate the usefulness of synthetic audit logs, we ran established baselines of event-based technique hunting and APT campaign detection using various synthetic audit logs.
elements.sent-2411-009: In addition, we show that a deep learning model trained on synthetic audit logs can detect previously unseen techniques within audit logs.
elements.sent-2411-010: APT campaigns, typically orchestrated by advanced threat actors with economic or political motives,involve multiple stages, including gaining initial access to a victim environment, remaining undetected for extended periods, exfiltrating sensitive data, and compromising system integrity.
elements.sent-2411-011: Despite progress in malware analysis~\cite{MAMBA,APILI , effective detection and mitigation of APT attacks remains a critical priority.}
elements.sent-2411-012: A key obstacle in APT threat studies is the lack of a widely recognized benchmark dataset~\cite{zepperle2022 , which is essential to develop AI/ML-based solutions and objectively evaluate methods.}
elements.sent-2411-013: This challenge motivates the study of synthetic audit log generation in this work.
elements.sent-2411-014: System audit logs, which record traces of program execution, play a crucial role in log analysis for threat detection.
elements.sent-2411-015: For example, MITRE ATT\&CK Evaluations~\cite{attack_eval provide emulation plans that allow cybersecurity providers to assess their product’s ability to detect simulated cyber attacks through system logs.}
elements.sent-2411-016: With the advancement of data-driven artificial intelligence methods, it has become increasingly effective in addressing certain cyber threats, such as attack analysis \cite{alsaheel2021atlas, ding2023airtag, cheng2023kairos , which offer the potential for timely Technique detection.}
elements.sent-2411-017: These approaches typically rely on high-quality labeled audit logs for model training.
elements.sent-2411-018: In addition, it allows for the enhancement and comparison of various methods.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, labeling large volumes of audit logs over long periods poses challenges, especially when APT attacks lack obvious indicators.
elements.sent-2411-020: Researchers often rely on publicly available datasets such as the DARPA Transparent Computing Engagement~\cite{darpa2tc , Operationally Transparent Cyber (OpTC)\cite{darpa2optc , and LANL’s dataset\cite{kent2015comprehensive , as well as smaller datasets that are rarely shared due to privacy and proprietary concerns.}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: This shortage of labeled logs limits the comprehensive testing of cybersecurity solutions and restricts the application of AI/ML in advancing cybersecurity studies~\cite{arp2022and, jacobs2022ai .}
elements.sent-2411-022: The absence of such audit logs motivates the development of methods for generating configurable synthetic audit logs that fulfill the following key requirements.
elements.sent-2411-023: Synthetic audit log should closely resemble the real audit logs.
elements.sent-2411-024: They should represent a broad spectrum of APT campaigns, including recent and emerging threats.
elements.sent-2411-025: Logs should include fine-grained labels such as specific Techniques or equivalents mapped to events in the audit log.
elements.sent-2411-026: Along with raw events, they should offer higher-level abstractions, facilitating easier understanding, examination, and communication.
elements.sent-2411-027: The framework should be able to generate logs of any duration and include any number of APT campaigns.
elements.sent-2411-028: The framework should be able to generate previously unseen logs, which would benefit model training and examination.
elements.sent-2411-029: This study introduces SAGA (Synthetic Audit Log Generation for APT Campaigns), a framework for generating configurable synthetic audit logs.
elements.sent-2411-030: SAGA is aligned with the MITRE ATT\&CK framework and terminologies, chosen for its openness, comprehensiveness, and availability.
elements.sent-2411-031: The generated synthetic audit log contains both benign and malicious (APT campaigns) audit logs, with benign logs representing normal system and user operations and malicious logs constructed from pre-defined Technique templates.
elements.sent-2411-032: Each APT campaign in the logs , comprising stages/tactics with corresponding techniques, which are reflected in the audit logs.
elements.sent-2411-033: The specific tasks of this study include:.
elements.sent-2411-035: Use a red team emulator to collect and label logs with technique identifiers based on the MITRE ATT\&CK framework.
elements.sent-2411-037: Develop the SAGA framework and workflow to address the aforementioned challenges.
elements.sent-2411-039: Create synthetic audit logs that combine APT attack scenarios and benign behaviors, resembling real data logs.
elements.sent-2411-041: Run existing systems using the SAGA synthetic audit log as input to demonstrate its effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-042: Furthermore, show that models trained on the synthetic audit log can successfully identify previously unseen malicious behaviors in new datasets.
elements.sent-2411-043: To demonstrate the usefulness of synthetic audit logs, we tested established baselines and the existing methods across various applications, including APT intrusion detection, technique hunting, and APT campaign detection.
elements.sent-2411-044: The objective of this empirical study is to demonstrate that SAGA synthetic audit logs can be used effectively and meaningfully in cybersecurity-related audit log applications.
elements.sent-2411-045: The focus is not on comparing the performance or goodness of these methods, as they target different objectives and operate in distinct contexts, making direct comparison uninformative.
elements.sent-2411-046: In this section, we introduce the audit log and its related issues using a running example of an APT campaign.
elements.sent-2411-047: System audit logs are records of system events such as file access, process creation and termination, and network access.
elements.sent-2411-048: Each system event depicts the interaction between two system entities, typically represented as \textless{ subject, operation, object\textgreater{ , where the subject refers to a process entity or a program, and the object represents a system entity such as a process, file, registry, or network socket.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The operation signifies the action performed by the subject on the object.
elements.sent-2411-050: For example, the event \textless{ WinRAR.exe, CreateFile, IOC\_09\_11.rar\textgreater{ indicates that the WinRAR process creates a file named IOC\_09\_11.rar.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: System events, along with their subjects and objects, include attributes such as meta-information, properties, and other details provided by the system, which can further describe the event and context.
elements.sent-2411-052: These details will be discussed in the subsequent section.
elements.sent-2411-053: System audit logs can be obtained using built-in kernel event logging mechanisms such as Windows Event Tracing (ETW) and Linux Audit, or through additional tools like Procmon, Sysmon, and CamFlow.

elements.sent-2411-080: The Red Team Platform, such as CALDERA~\cite{applebaum2016caldera,caldera and Atomic Red Team~\cite{atomic , simulates real-world adversaries in a controlled environment.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: In this study, we use CALDERA to emulate real-world attacks by adversaries.
elements.sent-2411-082: Our choice is based on its open source nature and alignment with the MITRE ATT\&CK framework.
elements.sent-2411-083: To deploy CALDERA, we configure a master server and set up Remote Access Tool (RAT) clients on a compromised host.
elements.sent-2411-084: An ability of CALDERA refers to a specific implementation of an ATT\&CK Technique, representing a discrete adversarial behavior.
elements.sent-2411-085: After acquiring audit logs from a red team emulator and process monitor, the next step is to extract the relevant attack patterns and assign appropriate Technique labels.
elements.sent-2411-086: CALDERA-generated reports provide details for each executed attack, including process IDs, commands used, and associated technique IDs.
elements.sent-2411-087: By mapping process IDs and commands to the corresponding audit logs, we can accurately assign technique ID to the relevant entries in the logs.
elements.sent-2411-088: However, not all events generated during an attack should be considered malicious and labeled with the Technique identifier, as some may represent background or system activities.
elements.sent-2411-089: To ensure accurate labeling, we define specific rules for each Technique attack pattern based on the MITRE ATT\&CK framework~\cite{strom2018mitre , helping to differentiate between events related to attacks and events not related to attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-090: In the case of T1566.001, the ability of T1566.001 involves using the Invoke-WebRequest cmdlet of PowerShell, which indicates network activity connecting to the GitHub website.
elements.sent-2411-091: Furthermore, the flag -OutFile and parameter \textbackslash\$env:TEMP\textbackslash PhishingAttachment.xlsm suggests that a file named PhishingAttachment.xlsm will be created on the target system.
elements.sent-2411-092: Only events exhibiting these specific malicious actions and artifacts are considered malicious for this particular ability, and custom labeling rules are crafted to capture this behavior.
elements.sent-2411-093: Each attack pattern corresponds to a specific instance of a Technique with particular parameter values.
elements.sent-2411-094: To enable the generation of a wider variety of attack patterns, it is essential to abstract these patterns into a more abstracted form.
elements.sent-2411-095: These abstractions can then be instantiated with legitimate parameter values, allowing the creation of new attack patterns.
elements.sent-2411-096: This approach facilitates greater flexibility and diversity in the generation of new attack instances.
elements.sent-2411-097: We abstract each attack pattern into an attack template, which specifies its prerequisites, outcomes, timings, and the corresponding stage and Technique in the attack lifecycle.
elements.sent-2411-098: This meta-information serves as a blueprint for generating diverse attack instances while maintaining consistency among the generated instances.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Generate Attack Pattern Template} \label{alg:generate_template} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \renewcommand{\algorithmicrequire}{ Input:} \renewcommand{\algorithmicensure}{ Output:} \REQUIRE Attack pattern $A$ and events $E$ \ENSURE Attack pattern template $P$ \STATE Let $TAB$ be the table that stores known system entities and their descriptors and categories. \FOR{each event $e$ in $E$} \FOR{each system entity $s$ in $E$} \IF{$s$ already used} \STATE Replace $s$ with its descriptor $d$ from $TAB$. \ELSE \STATE Find its descriptor $d$ and replace $s$. \STATE Set the descriptor as a prerequisite. \ENDIF \STATE {Set $d$ as an outcome if it still exists.} \STATE Insert $e$ into $P$. \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \RETURN $P$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-117: SAGA supports both random and user-specified APT campaign generation, and in this subsection, the random APT campaign generation is introduced.
elements.sent-2411-118: The generation of an APT campaign is conditioned on the adversary lifecycle, involving lifecycle stage transitions, attack template selections, and attack pattern instantiation.
elements.sent-2411-119: The transition process of the lifecycle stage determines the subsequent stage according to the current status.
elements.sent-2411-120: An example of an adversary Mandiant lifecycle is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:Running_example \circled{a .}}
elements.sent-2411-121: The attack template selection process chooses the attack templates for each stage, while the artifact substitution process instantiates the events for an attack pattern.
elements.sent-2411-122: As described in Section~\ref{sec: lifecycle , threat actors follow a systematic, strategic approach, executing actions sequentially.}
elements.sent-2411-123: An APT campaign begins with two initial stages: initial compromise (IC) and establish foothold (EF).
elements.sent-2411-124: This is followed by an incubation period consisting of four stages: escalate privileges (EP), internal reconnaissance (IR), move laterally (ML), and maintain presence (MP).
elements.sent-2411-125: The incubation period may occur once or multiple times, and within each incubation period, individual stages may be omitted or repeated.
elements.sent-2411-126: Finally, the last stage, complete mission (CM), is executed once or not at all, marking the successful completion of the attack.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Synthetic Audit Log Generation} \label{alg:campaign_gen} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE ~~ Lifecyle $\{LC\}$, Attack pattern templates $\{TQ\}$, Benign audit log $\{BA\}$, Background events $\{BG\}$ \ENSURE ~~ Synthetic audit events $SA$ \WHILE {while $LC_i$ in {$LC$} exists} \FOR{each $stage$ in $LC_i$} \STATE {// Include an attack pattern template $TQ$ in the $stage$. Line 8 of Table~\ref{tbl:CFG}}. \STATE $MA$ = $BE+TQ+MA$ $\vert$ $BE+TQ$ \STATE {where $TQ$ is an instantiated template, as Line 10 of Table~\ref{tbl:CFG}. $BE$ is a short list of benign events.} \ENDFOR \ENDWHILE {~~//Obtain all malicious audit logs $\{ MA \}$} \STATE \STATE $SA$ = $BG$ \FOR{each $BA_i$ in $\{BA\}$} \STATE {// Perform $C_{b}(SA, BA_i )$, as Line 1 of Table~\ref{tbl:CFG}}. \FOR{each $evt$ in $\{ BA_i \}$} \STATE $evt.start = {BA_i}.start + evt.relative$ \ENDFOR {~// Insert event $evt$ to synthetic audit log.} \STATE $SA$ = $SA \cup BA_i$ \ENDFOR {~// Insert benign audit log $BE_i$.} \FOR{each $MA_i$ in $\{MA\}$} \STATE {// Perform $C_{m}(SA,MA)$, as Line 1 of Table~\ref{tbl:CFG}}. \FOR{each $TQ_j$ in $\{ MA_i \}$} \FOR{each $evt$ in $\{ TQ_j \}$} \STATE $evt.start = {TQ_{j-1}}.end + TQ_{j}.lapse + evt.relative$ \ENDFOR {~// Insert event $evt$ to synthetic audit log.} \STATE $SA$ = $SA \cup TQ_j$ \ENDFOR {~// Insert technique $TQ$ to synthetic audit log.} \ENDFOR {~// Insert malicious audit log $MA$ to synthetic audit log.} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-141: In Subsection \ref{sec: template , we discuss the creation of attack templates through the generalization of system entities.}
elements.sent-2411-142: While instantiating an attack pattern template, these generalized system entities are replaced by appropriate and meaningful artifacts (or a hyponym) to form a validated attack pattern.
elements.sent-2411-143: This approach enables the generation of a wide range of attack instances.
elements.sent-2411-144: There are two methods for collecting the hyponyms of generalized system entities.
elements.sent-2411-145: The first method involves using artifacts collected from real-world data, such as malware names and attack-related file names and registry paths.
elements.sent-2411-146: To gather these artifacts, we leveraged VX-Underground~\cite{vxheaven and VirusTotal~\cite{virustotal .}}
elements.sent-2411-147: VX-Underground provides approximately 10,000 malware hash values used associated with APT campaigns over the past five years.
elements.sent-2411-148: We then queried each hash on VirusTotal to extract artifacts from behavioral reports.
elements.sent-2411-149: These artifacts were then mapped to the generalized system entities in the attack template for later instantiation.
elements.sent-2411-150: For example, by querying VirusTotal using a sample hash value 78a3e4702d9fc13b2ef917211cd65b44, we can extract a file \%APPDATA\%\textbackslash $...$ \textbackslash Startup\textbackslash twainResolver.lnk.
elements.sent-2411-151: The dropped file, twainResolver.lnk, classified under the system entity type file, can be used as an email attachment in an attack behavior such as Spearphishing Attachment.
elements.sent-2411-152: The second source of artifacts, such as usernames and process IDs, which are indicated with $*$ Table~\ref{tab:hypernym , is generated using the Faker library in Python~\cite{faker .}}
elements.sent-2411-153: Faker efficiently generates realistic-looking random data that mimics real-world information.
elements.sent-2411-154: These random artifacts are used to populate attack instances within the templates.
elements.sent-2411-155: Once attack patterns are generated for all stages as a chronological sequence of labeled events, the prerequisites for every attack pattern must be satisfied to validate the attack sequence, which is then considered an APT campaign.

elements.section-2411-004: Usefulness Study of Synthetic Audit Log
elements.sent-2411-175: In this section, we evaluate the usefulness of synthetic audit logs for various detection methods in different APT attack scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-176: First, we show the variety of synthetic audit logs that SAGA can produce, from those that mimic real-world APT attacks (denoted as the C Group) to randomly generated logs (denoted as the G group), and finally to composite both groups (denoted as the M group).
elements.sent-2411-177: Next, we assess whether state-of-the-art audit log-based and provenance-based malicious detection methods can effectively use the synthetic audit log as their target datasets.
elements.sent-2411-178: In addition, we discern the performance and operation differences of these methods when applying their original datasets versus various synthetic data logs.
elements.sent-2411-179: Note that the objective of this study is not to identify the best-performing methods or models, as each approach has its own specific focus, design considerations, and inherent limitations.
elements.sent-2411-180: This section begins by outlining the settings for the empirical study, including the synthesized datasets, labeling, metrics, and machine hardware and software configurations.
elements.sent-2411-181: Our analysis focuses on three sets of empirical studies to demonstrate the usefulness of the synthesized attack datasets: 1) assessing the effectiveness of APT intrusion detection by, 2) evaluating the accuracy of Technique hunting, and 3) gauging the effectiveness of campaign attribution.
elements.sent-2411-182: Finally, we will conduct a case study to evaluate the effect of model training using synthetic audit logs to test the model on an unseen dataset.
elements.sent-2411-183: The experiments utilize synthetic audit log datasets generated by SAGA (hereafter referred to as the SAGA dataset), which is publicly available at ~\cite{saga_dataset .}
elements.sent-2411-184: A synthetic audit log, as described in Section~\ref{sec: generativeThreatModel , includes both benign behaviors and malicious APT attack scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-185: Similarly to previous research~\cite{manzoor2016steampot, alsaheel2021atlas , the benign audit logs cover a variety of everyday activities, such as watching YouTube videos, downloading and compiling source code from GitHub, writing and sending emails, running Python programs, playing video games, reading news articles, and composing Word documents.}
elements.sent-2411-186: This diversity ensures that the synthetic audit logs accurately reflect typical user behavior.
elements.sent-2411-187: For malicious events, we prepared 169 abilities executed on the MITRE CALDERA~\cite{caldera red-team emulator to generate labeled malicious audit events.}
elements.sent-2411-188: These events were then processed into attack pattern templates for further synthetic audit log generation.
elements.sent-2411-189: Each of the 169 attack pattern templates was instantiated with artifact replacements, as described in Section~\ref{sec: template , to form the datasets used in the empirical study.}
elements.sent-2411-190: The training dataset for Technique hunting consists of 16,900 attack patterns, each of which is an instantiation of a attack pattern template.
elements.sent-2411-191: This dataset is split into training, validation, and test sets in an 8:1:1 ratio.
elements.sent-2411-192: Given the limited availability of audit logs containing labeled with Techniques, we used the attack pattern templates to create APT campaigns.
elements.sent-2411-193: These scenarios include eight APT attack campaigns (``known'' group) based on cyber threat intelligence reports from real-world APT campaigns, including Higaisa~\cite{Malwarebytes2020Higaisa , admin338~\cite{Mandiant2015admin@338 , APT28~\cite{APT28 , FIN7~\cite{Mandiant2017FIN7 , CobaltGroup~\cite{ptsecurity2017Cobalt , Gamaredon~\cite{CERTEE2021Gamaredon , Patchwork~\cite{Cymmetria2016Patchwork , and GorgonGroup~\cite{Unit2017Gorgon .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-194: Moreover, using SAGA random generation capability, we produced 20 APT campaigns (``random generated group'').
elements.sent-2411-195: From these 28 APT campaigns, we randomly selected 3 to create new composite scenarios involving multiple APT campaigns targeting a single victim host, resulting in 10 composite campaigns (``composite'' group).
elements.sent-2411-196: To demonstrate the versatility and flexibility of SAGA, each of the above APT campaigns was generated over three different time periods: 15 minutes, 1 hour, and 1 day.. Labeling.
elements.sent-2411-197: For each generated APT campaign, SAGA labels malicious events with APT stages, Techniques, abilities, manipulated system entities, and other operation-related attributes.
elements.sent-2411-198: In addition, it includes threat actors, when applicable.
elements.sent-2411-199: Malicious audit events are labeled using the BIO2 scheme~\cite{sang2003introduction covering 7 attack stages, 80 Techniques, and 169 abilities, as shown in Table S1 of Supplementary Material A.. Evaluation Metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-200: We adopt three evaluation metrics for different tasks: APT intrusion detection, Technique hunting, and APT campaign attribution.
elements.sent-2411-201: The APT intrusion detection task aims to identify any APT intrusion or anomalies within the dataset under examination.
elements.sent-2411-202: Technique hunting seeks to identify Techniques violated, based on MITRE ATT\&CK definitions.
elements.sent-2411-203: APT campaign attribution aims to determine the most probable APT campaign present in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-204: To evaluate performance, we use standard classification metrics: precision (P), recall (R), F1 score (F1), and accuracy (ACC).
elements.sent-2411-205: For APT intrusion detection, we frame it as a binary classification problem, determining whether a malicious event exists in the audit log.
elements.sent-2411-206: Since different target systems or methods apply their own tests to detect APT intrusion on an event, a graph, or a time window interval, we adapt to their respective evaluation design to assess performance.
elements.sent-2411-207: Technique hunting is treated as a multi-class classification task, where each classifier determines whether a specific Technique has been employed in one or more events.
elements.sent-2411-208: For APT campaign attribution, we analyze the Techniques identified from the Technique hunting task to determine the most likely APT campaign for the audit log.
elements.sent-2411-209: We rank the ground truth against the predictions and employ Top-K measurement to evaluate the performance of the APT campaign attribution.
elements.sent-2411-210: Established Baselines For APT intrusion detection, we demonstrate the usefulness of generated attack datasets using three types of systems: event-based (AirTag~\cite{ding2023airtag ), graph-based (Unicorn~\cite{han2020unicorn ), and time window-based (KAIROS~\cite{cheng2023kairos ) intrusion detection systems.}}}
elements.sent-2411-211: The reasons for choosing the three systems are not only because they are established baselines, but also because their codes are publicly available and the systems can be installed and executed without issues.
elements.sent-2411-212: For Technique hunting, we used the Sigma rules~\cite{sigma , an open signature-based rulebase contributed by numerous security practitioners, and SFM~\cite{huang2024cascadeapproachaptcampaign , a deep learning approach that provides Technique hunting functionality.}}
elements.sent-2411-213: Sigma rules help bridge the gap caused by the lack of a standardized benchmark to represent fine-grained attack patterns, enabling the identification of relevant log events.
elements.sent-2411-214: A major portion of these rules align with the MITRE ATT\&CK framework with 70 rules mapped to the Techniques identified in this study.
elements.sent-2411-215: SFM integrates three key technologies: anomaly detection to mitigate the impact of data imbalance, a multi-class classifier for Technique hunting, and subgraph matching for APT campaign attribution.
elements.sent-2411-216: SFM was selected because it was one of the few systems specifically designed to address both the Technique hunting task and APT campaign attribution, making it particularly suited for the evaluation of complex attack scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-217: All models and methods were installed with configurations as close as possible to those described in their original publications.
elements.sent-2411-218: Code modifications were only made to ensure compatibility with the structure of the synthetic audit logs.

elements.sent-2411-223: In this section, we provide a more detailed discussion on the considerations for generating the APT dataset, focusing primarily on generation guidance, attack scenarios, and duration.. Generation Guidance.
elements.sent-2411-224: SAGA offers flexibility in generating APT campaigns, based on user specifications, randomly, or through a combination of both.
elements.sent-2411-225: For example, SAGA generates eight known APT campaigns (denoted from C1 to C8) following user specifications, with their lifecycles and Techniques presented in Table~\ref{tab:known_campaigns .}
elements.sent-2411-226: To further illustrate SAGA's versatility, we also used its random generation capabilities to create 20 additional campaigns (denoted from G1 to G20), showcasing its capacity to create varied attack scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-227: The lifecycles and Techniques of these randomly generated campaigns are presented in Table S2 of Supplementary Material A, while the detailed statistics, including sizes, number of entities, and number of events, are provided in Table S3 also in Supplementary Material A.
elements.sent-2411-228: This flexibility highlights the scalability and adaptability of our approach in simulating a wide range of cyberattack scenarios, enabling the creation of customized and diverse testing environments to thoroughly evaluate detection methods.. Attack Scenario.
elements.sent-2411-229: We introduce two variations in attack scenarios: one involving a single campaign targeting the system and another featuring three simultaneous campaigns.
elements.sent-2411-230: This variation in attack composition allows for a comprehensive assessment of detection methods in more complex, multi-campaign situations.
elements.sent-2411-231: For instance, the M1 attack scenario consists of three separate attacks, Higaisa, G1, and admin338, each employing distinct Techniques and targeting the same compromised host.
elements.sent-2411-232: Additional details on the ten generated composite campaigns can be found in Table S4 in Supplementary Material A, due to the constraints of the manuscript space.. Attack Duration.
elements.sent-2411-233: To account for the variability in the duration of the attack, we generated synthetic audit logs with durations of 15 minutes, 1 hour, and 1 day.
elements.sent-2411-234: Although SAGA can create logs of any length, we limited the maximum duration to 1 day in this study, considering the computational overhead of the intrusion detection systems discussed in Section~\ref{sec: intrusion system Table~\ref{tab:known_campaigns_stat , Table S3 and Table S4 in Supplementary Material A, presents statistical descriptions of logs for each duration.}}
elements.sent-2411-235: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:known_campaigns_stat , the average number of events increases with log length: 910,711 for 15-minute audit logs, 1,184,512.5 for 1-hour audit logs, and 14,263,393.25 for 1-day audit logs.}
elements.sent-2411-236: This variability reflects the evolving nature of APT attacks, which are not constrained by fixed timeframes, highlighting SAGA ability to generate audit logs of various durations.
elements.sent-2411-237: It also allows for a more thorough examination of how different detection methods perform over varying time intervals.

elements.sent-2411-266: Technique hunting is the task of identifying potential threats, specifically Techniques, as defined by the MITRE ATT\&CK framework.
elements.sent-2411-267: SAGA follows Algorithm \ref{alg:campaign_gen to generate attack campaigns, where each event is labeled with its corresponding Technique identifier.}
elements.sent-2411-268: This structured synthetic audit log allows detection methods to effectively perform Technique hunting.
elements.sent-2411-269: The approaches are used for Technique hunting; one is SFM and the other is using Sigma rules.
elements.sent-2411-270: The performance results of Technique hunting for each APT attack campaign are reported in Table~\ref{tab:ttp_hunting that details are given in Table S9 in the Supplementary Material C.}
elements.sent-2411-271: The results indicate significant performance distinctions between SFM and Sigma rules, regardless of the generation method, attack scenario, or duration.
elements.sent-2411-272: SFM is capable of recognizing attack patterns by learning from synthetic data, whereas Sigma rules are limited to detecting attack patterns that match pre-defined signatures.
elements.sent-2411-273: This underscores the importance of the generated datasets in facilitating the advancement of learning-based model methodologies.
elements.sent-2411-274: However, both methods showed significant performance fluctuations across various attack scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-275: For example, the average standard deviations of precision during the 15-min scenarios were 33.92\% and 32.25\% for SFM and Sigma rules, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-276: Several factors contributed to this variability.
elements.sent-2411-277: Firstly, there was a high incidence of false positives associated with specific attack behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-278: Secondly, the substantial imbalance between the number of malicious events and the overwhelming prevalence of benign activities skewed the evaluation of attack behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-279: Lastly, benign activities and malicious events were intertwined and exhibited different patterns in various datasets, complicating the recognition of attack patterns.
elements.sent-2411-280: For instance, recognizing T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer (ITT3) requires identifying the following sequence of audit events within interleaved benign event sequences: \textless{ browser.exe, TCP connect, XXX.com\textgreater{ , \textless{ browser.exe, FileCreate, payload.exe\textgreater{ , and \textless{ xxx, Process Create, payload.exe\textgreater{ .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-281: This sequence indicates that a malicious payload was downloaded from an external C2 server and executed on a compromised machine.
elements.sent-2411-282: These malicious events are causally correlated and in audit log, they were intertwined with benign and background events, making the recognition of this Technique particularly challenging.
elements.sent-2411-283: On the other hand, while Sigma rules perform efficiently, they are based on contributions from human experts and may not encompass all attack behaviors, such as T1055.002 Portable Executable Injection (PEI) and T1491 Defacement (DF2) identified in this empirical study.
elements.sent-2411-284: As a result, certain malicious behaviors went undetected in the APT campaigns C-6, C-8, G-11, and G-13.
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=1\columnwidth]{images/Attack_Campaign.jpg} \caption{The top $k$ ranking results of APT campaign detection by SFM.} \label{fig:Attack_Campaign}
elements.sent-2411-285: APT campaign detection involves identifying a potential APT campaign embedded in an audit log, which is characterized by a series of coordinated attack patterns.
elements.sent-2411-286: In line with the MITRE ATT\&CK framework, an APT campaign can be described as a sequence of Techniques.
elements.sent-2411-287: SFM leverages this structure to detect the most likely APT campaign by comparing the discovered Techniques ($G_q$) against a pool of known APT campaigns ($G_c$).
elements.sent-2411-288: In this empirical study, $G_c$ includes 8 known campaigns and 30 randomly generated APT campaigns.
elements.sent-2411-289: Figure~\ref{fig:Attack_Campaign illustrates the ranking of each SFM-identified campaign over three different durations, 15 minutes, 1 hour, and 1 day.}
elements.sent-2411-290: SFM successfully identified most campaigns within the top 3 rankings for single campaign groups (C and G), across all three time durations.
elements.sent-2411-291: In the 15-minute scenarios, 65\% of the campaigns were ranked first.
elements.sent-2411-292: One reason for this success could be that the temporal causal relationships is established among the Techniques discovered by SFM, which effectively distinguishes the most probable campaign from others.
elements.sent-2411-293: Furthermore, SFM demonstrated resilience to discrepancies between the discovered ability graph $G_q$ and the campaign Techniques $G_c$.
elements.sent-2411-294: However, the M group, consisting of multiple campaigns in a single scenario, posed a greater challenge compared to the groups of single campaigns.
elements.sent-2411-295: In addition, the results further suggest that as the duration of audit log grows, the complexity of identifying APT campaigns also increases.
elements.sent-2411-296: In conclusion, this evaluation highlights the potential of SAGA in effectively supporting the detection of APT campaigns.
elements.subsection-2411-013: A Case Study of Unseen APT Campaign
elements.sent-2411-297: In this section, we investigate the effectiveness of SAGA synthetic audit log datasets in training an APT campaign model to be applied to previously unseen APT campaigns, even when the campaign audit logs follow a different format and organization.
elements.sent-2411-298: Specifically, SFM was trained using SAGA datasets and tested on the DARPA TC E3 Fivedirection dataset to detect attack behaviors (i.e., Techniques) within the logs.
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{images/DARPA_sc.png} \caption{The DARPA TC E3 Fivedirection attack graph} \label{fig:darpa_e3}
elements.sent-2411-299: The E3 Fivedirection dataset includes a complex attack scenario (4.4 Phishing Email with Excel Macro~\cite{darpa2data ).}
elements.sent-2411-300: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:darpa_e3 , the attacker used a phishing attachment to lure the victim into downloading and executing malware that triggered subsequent malicious activities, including establishing a remote command shell and gathering various system information.}
elements.sent-2411-301: From the detection results, it shows that SFM was able to successfully identify part of the attack sequence.
elements.sent-2411-302: For example, during the initial compromise phase, the SFM successfully flagged the system events corresponding to the victim downloading the phishing attachment, \textless{ thunderbird.exe, WriteFile, BoviaBenefitsOE.xlsm\textgreater{ , as T1566.001 Spearphishing Attachment.}}
elements.sent-2411-303: Additionally, during the Establish Foothold phase, the system events corresponding to the execution of the phishing attachment, \textless{ Excel.exe, CreateFile, BoviaBenefitsOE.xlsm\textgreater{ , were also correctly flagged as T1204.002 User Execution: Malicious File.}}
elements.sent-2411-304: However, some attack behaviors were not correctly identified by SFM.
elements.sent-2411-305: For example, during the Establish Foothold phase, when the attacker used PowerShell to download a malicious payload (update.ps1) from a C2 server, SFM successfully marked the system events related to the download activity, \textless{ powershell.exe, CreateFile, update.ps1\textgreater{ , as T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer, but it failed to include the system events, PowerShell connection to the C2 server, \textless{ powershell.exe, TCP connect, 208.75.117.5\textgreater{ in T1105.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-306: This limitation could be attributed to gaps in the SAGA dataset’s representation of certain attack behaviors, or SFM's difficulty in detecting subtle variations in event sequences.
elements.sent-2411-307: However, these results highlight the practical value of SAGA datasets for training detection models and provide a solid foundation for refining dataset generation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-308: They also underscore the need for further improvements in APT campaign detection models to enhance their capability and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-309: However, these results highlight the practical value of the SAGA dataset in supporting practical cybersecurity applications and providing a solid foundation for future improvements to dataset generation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-310: Existing Benchmarks for Host-based Intrusion Detection.
elements.sent-2411-311: As noted in the survey by~\cite{zipperle2022provenance , the majority of studies on host-based intrusion detection have relied on self-constructed attack scenarios for evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-312: Some works have utilized existing public datasets such as CERT’s dataset~\cite{glasser2013bridging , LANL’s dataset~\cite{kent2015comprehensive , StreamSpot~\cite{manzoor2016steampot ADFA~\cite{creech2014ADFA , and DARPA Transparent Computing (TC)\cite{darpa2tc , as well as OpTC~\cite{darpa2optc .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-313: CERT's dataset provides synthetic background data alongside malicious operations, such as logon and email activities, carried out by multiple threat actors across five distinct attack scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-314: LANL's dataset consists of 58 consecutive days of event data collected from LANL's corporate computer network, including logon events, process events, DNS lookups, network flow events, and a set of well-defined red teaming events representing malicious activities.
elements.sent-2411-315: StreamSpot includes one attack activity—a drive-by download initiated by visiting a malicious URL that gains root access to the visiting host—along with five benign activities forming three scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-316: However, these datasets primarily focus on event action types (i.e., operations) and timestamps, lacking critical details regarding the underlying system entities involved in the audit events.
elements.sent-2411-317: This absence limits their ability to clearly describe the full context of each event, which is essential for comprehensive attack pattern analysis and detection.
elements.sent-2411-318: ADFA comprises three datasets: ADFA-LD (Linux), ADFA-WD (Windows), and ADFA-WD-SAA (Windows for Stealth Attacks).
elements.sent-2411-319: Among these, ADFA-WD is the most relevant to SAGA.
elements.sent-2411-320: However, it only contains DLL traces of processes collected by Procmon and includes twelve zero-day attacks executed on a host.
elements.sent-2411-321: DARPA's TC program included five engagements and the OpTC dataset.
elements.sent-2411-322: The E3 and E5 engagements, featuring two and eight multi-stage APTs respectively, are widely used for intrusion detection, alongside benign background activity.
elements.sent-2411-323: The OpTC dataset, created to assess scalability, simulated a three-day APT on a 1,000-host test network during a two-week experiment.
elements.sent-2411-324: However, these datasets lack thorough documentation and labels, which limits their usefulness for precisely evaluating attack behaviors and audit events.
elements.sent-2411-326: While there are some publicly available attack datasets, few research focus on constructing datasets for simulating multi-stage APT attacks~\cite{myneni2020dapt, berady2022pwnjutsu, myneni2023unraveled, LADEMU .}
elements.sent-2411-327: To steal organization data, DAPT2020~\cite{myneni2020dapt imitated the exploited web vulnerability in a production network, and Unraveled~\cite{myneni2023unraveled created skeletons of user profiles to emulate user behavior based on predefined job pipelines.}}
elements.sent-2411-328: These datasets encompass network traffic and multi-host log information, covering limited attacks (i.e., 10 and 15) within attack stages over a fixed collection duration (i.e., 1 week or 6 weeks), In contrast, our generated APT dataset includes and labels 7 attack stages, 80 techniques, and 169 abilities, offering configurable attack patterns and collection duration on single-host audit events.
elements.sent-2411-329: This flexibility allows for more detailed and customizable simulation of attack behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-330: PWNJUTSU~\cite{berady2022pwnjutsu and LADEMU~\cite{LADEMU considered adversaries' TTPs, defining the operational semantics of Techniques as specifications for procedures.}}
elements.sent-2411-331: PWNJUTSU utilized a semi-automated approach where each attack step was crafted by professional human Red Teamers, providing a highly skilled yet partially manual campaign construction process.
elements.sent-2411-332: In contrast, LADEMU offered a proof-of-concept implementation using the red-team emulator Caldera and demonstrated the use of a single attack scenario, APT29.
elements.sent-2411-333: Our study introduces a workflow of campaign generation with event logs, enabling fully automated APT simulations based on TTPs specified in the MITRE ATT\&CK framework.
elements.sent-2411-334: This approach eliminates the need for manual intervention, ensuring more consistent and scalable attack simulations.. Host-based Provenance analysis.
elements.sent-2411-335: Learning-based provenance analysis methods have shown notable improvements in the detection of intrusions and have accelerated the investigative process of various attacks~\cite{zipperle2022provenance, han2020unicorn, alsaheel2021atlas, zengy2022shadewatcher, ding2023airtag, cheng2023kairos, jia2023magic, sharif2024drsec .}
elements.sent-2411-336: These learning-based models employ a range of representation learning techniques to encode provenance graphs, enabling effective anomaly detection.
elements.sent-2411-337: Examples include graph representation methods used in Unicorn~\cite{han2020unicorn , Kairos~\cite{cheng2023kairos , and MAGIC~\cite{jia2023magic ; translation-based embedding techniques in WATSON~\cite{zeng2021watson and ShadeWatcher~\cite{zengy2022shadewatcher ; and language models in AirTag~\cite{ding2023airtag and DrSec~\cite{sharif2024drsec .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-338: Our research demonstrates that applying event-based~\cite{ding2023airtag and graph-based intrusion detection~\cite{han2020unicorn, cheng2023kairos to our synthesized campaign datasets results in effective detection of intrusion events.. Technique Hunting.}}
elements.sent-2411-339: HOLMES~\cite{milajerdi2019holmes generates detection signals by matching 16 predefined attack patterns and constructing a high-level scenario graph embedded with TTPs to indicate APT campaign activities.}
elements.sent-2411-340: RapSheet~\cite{hassan2020tactical creates a tactical provenance graph using 67 rules within a commercial EDR tool, aligning it with MITRE ATT\&CK.}
elements.sent-2411-341: KRYSTAL~\cite{kurniawan2022krystal leverages Sigma rules~\cite{sigma2 to detect known attack patterns, converting them into SPARQL queries for threat detection.}}
elements.sent-2411-342: Although these rule-based approaches are effective, their manual creation and upkeep are resource-intensive.
elements.sent-2411-343: Our study addresses this limitation by introducing a flexible approach to APT dataset generation using a red-team emulator for audit event collection, enabling a learning-based methodology.
elements.sent-2411-344: Additionally, we propose a straightforward method (SFM) for identifying attack patterns, providing a baseline for future research.
elements.sent-2411-345: This study presents a generative APT campaign framework, SAGA, designed to synthesize synthetic audit logs for nearly any configuration.
elements.sent-2411-346: These logs support the training of various machine learning-based APT-related detection machine learning models and facilitate the evaluation of various APT-related detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-347: To demonstrate the usefulness of SAGA’s synthetic datasets, a collection of empirical studies were conducted across multiple established baselines for APT intrusion detection, Technique hunting, and APT campaign detection.
elements.sent-2411-348: Although each has specific strengths, they also have limitations.
elements.sent-2411-349: A widely accepted dataset, like the synthetic audit log generated by SAGA, could play a crucial role in improving the accuracy and robustness of these detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-350: However, the quality of SAGA datasets depends on access to extensive, high-quality campaign knowledge and diverse Technique implementations.
elements.sent-2411-351: The current implementation of SAGA uses 169 abilities and 80 Techniques, with ongoing efforts to expand these numbers to enhance the usefulness of SAGA datasets and cover more sophisticated APT campaigns.
elements.sent-2411-352: In this work, the Windows operating system is used as the target environment; however, the concept can be readily applied to other platforms as well.
